title: Secure Authentication — Password Spray Pattern in Entra Sign-Ins
exclusions: Approved maintenance window access; automated service account operations per register; break-glass access during declared incidents; access within recertification cycle timing; project-based temporary elevation.
evidence: Access governance report showing recertification completion rates, privilege changes, approval chain documentation, and anomalous access investigation outcomes.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.5 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.28: Secure Authentication — Password Spray Pattern in Entra Sign-Ins.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.28' running on mscs:azure:signinlog, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Grant a test user elevated access to a controlled system without a corresponding approval record in the access management system; verify the monitoring search detects the unapproved privilege within 1 hour and generates an alert with user, system, and missing approval details.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Grant a test user access following the full approval workflow with manager sign-off, risk assessment, and time-limited duration recorded; verify no access violation alert fires and the access appears as properly authorised in the dashboard.

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
spl:
index=azure sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog earliest=-24h
| bin _time span=10m
| stats dc(userPrincipalName) as users, count as attempts, sum(eval(if(status.errorCode!=0,1,0))) as fails by _time, IPAddress
| eval fail_rate=fails/attempts
| where users>20 AND fail_rate>0.7
description: Detects many distinct usernames from one IP with high failure ratio supporting secure authentication monitoring.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes azure and sourcetypes mscs:azure:signinlog. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Tune thresholds; (2) integrate with risk-based CA; (3) block IP at perimeter optional. Indexes required: `index=azure`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Scheduled maintenance window access** — Administrative access during documented maintenance windows with change tickets is pre-approved operational activity.

2. **Automated service account patterns** — Service accounts performing scheduled tasks generate high-frequency access patterns that are normal operational behaviour per the automation register.

3. **Break-glass emergency access** — Emergency access during declared incidents with post-incident review and documented justification follows the approved escalation process.

4. **Access review cycle timing** — Users retaining access between scheduled access review dates are within the approved recertification cadence per the access governance policy.

5. **Temporary elevated privileges** — Time-bounded privilege escalation for approved projects or maintenance activities with documented start and end dates.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `azure` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.8.5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.28: Secure Authentication — Password Spray Pattern in Entra Sign-Ins." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `azure` under `mscs:azure:signinlog`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Tune thresholds. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 2:** integrate with risk-based CA. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** block IP at perimeter optional. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `users > 20`. For "Secure Authentication — Password Spray Pattern in Entra Sign-Ins," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `20` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by users
| stats avg(users) as mean, perc50(users) as p50, perc90(users) as p90, perc99(users) as p99, max(users) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `20` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `dc()` aggregation over the last 24 hours is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.28: Secure Authentication — Password Spray Pattern in Entra Sign]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.28,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=azure sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `IPAddress`, `attempts`, `fail_rate`, `fails`, `users`. Run:

```spl
index=azure sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, IPAddress, attempts, fail_rate, fails, users
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `users`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(users) as p50 perc90(users) as p90 perc99(users) as p99 max(users) as hi
```

The current threshold `20` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.28: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Scatter (the key fields):** Plots two dimensions simultaneously; best for spotting outliers that multi-variable thresholds would miss.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Secure Authentication — Password Spray Pattern in Entra Sign-Ins. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
